questions.prompt: Your per-IP rate limit blocked a major corporate customer behind a single egress IP. They're now angry. Right fix?

choices.text: Add per-session rate limiting (cookie-based) so multiple users behind one IP each get their own budget. Raise the per-IP limit. Optionally allowlist known corporate egress IPs. Per-IP only is too blunt for shared-NAT scenarios — combine dimensions.

questions.explanation: Per-IP only fails on shared-egress networks (corporate, mobile carriers, VPNs). Add per-session or per-user dimensions to distinguish individuals behind shared IPs. The combination catches abuse without burning legitimate users.

questions.prompt: Layered defense for an auth endpoint should include what?

choices.text: Multiple layers stacked: ASN block on known-bad networks, bot score < 30 challenge, per-IP rate limit (e.g. 10 POSTs/min), Turnstile or CAPTCHA on the form itself, backend account-lock on repeated failures. Each layer catches what the previous missed; credential stuffing attempts blow up at one of them.

questions.explanation: Defense in depth on auth. Credential stuffers use residential proxies (high IP diversity), botnets (low Bot Scores), and dictionary attacks (many attempts per account). Each layer addresses a specific vector. Single-layer defenses fail; stacked ones don't.
